title: Asking Questions About Stories
introBody: Good readers ask questions while they read. Asking questions helps us understand the story better. Today we will practice asking and answering questions about stories!
introImage: A curious cartoon child with a big thought bubble containing a question mark, sitting at a desk with a colorful open book.
teachBody: When we read a story, we can ask questions like: Who is the story about? What happened? Why did it happen? The answers are hiding right in the story!
teachImage: A friendly cartoon detective fox with a magnifying glass looking at clues in a big open storybook.
exampleBody: Read this: Mia lost her red mitten at school. She looked everywhere and found it under her desk. Question: Where did Mia find her mitten? Answer: Under her desk! See? The answer was right in the story.
exampleImage: A smiling cartoon girl finding a red mitten under a wooden school desk, with a happy surprised expression.
tipBody: When you need to answer a question, go back and reread the story. Look for the words that match the question. The answer is always in the words!
tipImage: A cartoon child wearing a superhero cape, holding a book and pointing at highlighted words on a page.
practiceBody: Awesome! You know how to ask and answer questions about a story. Now let's try some practice questions. Read carefully and you will do great!
practiceImage: A confident cartoon child giving a thumbs up, standing next to a stack of colorful books with gold stars floating around.

title: Retelling Stories and Lessons
introBody: Have you ever told a friend about a story you read? That is called retelling! When we retell, we share what happened and what the story teaches us.
introImage: Two cartoon children sitting on a rug, one excitedly telling a story with hands waving while the other listens with wide eyes.
teachHeading: How Do We Retell a Story?
teachBody: To retell a story, think about what happened first, next, and last. Then ask yourself: What lesson did the characters learn? That is the message of the story!
teachImage: Three colorful stepping stones labeled "First," "Next," and "Last" leading across a stream, with a cartoon rabbit hopping along them.
exampleBody: A turtle wanted to win a race. He kept going slowly and never gave up. The fast rabbit took a nap and lost! The lesson is: Keep trying and don't give up. That is how we find the message!
exampleImage: A cartoon turtle crossing a finish line with a big smile while a rabbit sleeps under a tree in the background.
tipBody: After you read, close the book and tell the story in your own words. Use "first, next, then, last" to keep the events in order. Don't forget the lesson!
tipImage: A cartoon child holding up four fingers counting story events, with a small thought bubble showing a light bulb for the lesson.
practiceBody: Great job! You know how to retell a story and find the lesson. Now let's practice with some questions. Think about what happens and what it teaches us!
practiceImage: A cartoon child on a stage retelling a story to a group of smiling animal friends sitting in tiny chairs.

title: Characters, Settings, and Events
introBody: Every story has characters, a setting, and events. Characters are who the story is about. The setting is where and when it happens. Events are what happens!
introImage: A cartoon bear, a forest background, and a campfire scene arranged like puzzle pieces fitting together.
teachHeading: How Do We Find These Story Parts?
teachBody: Ask yourself: Who is in this story? Where does it take place? What happens to the characters? Look for details that describe each part!
teachImage: A cartoon owl teacher pointing at a chalkboard with three columns labeled "Who," "Where," and "What Happened" with cute doodles under each.
exampleBody: Read this: Ben and his dog Rusty played at the beach all morning. They built a big sand castle together. Character: Ben and Rusty. Setting: the beach. Event: They built a sand castle!
exampleImage: A cartoon boy and a golden dog building a sand castle on a sunny beach with ocean waves in the background.
tipBody: Use details from the story to describe the characters, setting, and events. Don't just say "a boy." Say "a boy named Ben who loves dogs!"
tipImage: A cartoon child with a notebook writing descriptive words, surrounded by floating labels like "kind," "sunny," and "exciting."
practiceBody: You are ready to find characters, settings, and events! Let's try some practice questions. Pay attention to the details in each story!
practiceImage: Three cartoon children each holding a sign: one says "Characters," one says "Settings," and one says "Events," all smiling.

title: Story Feelings and Senses
introBody: Some words in stories make us feel happy, sad, or excited. Other words help us see, hear, smell, taste, or touch things in our minds. These are special words!
introImage: A cartoon child reading a book with colorful swirls of images rising from the pages: a sun, music notes, flowers, and raindrops.
teachHeading: How Do We Find Feeling Words?
teachBody: Look for words that describe feelings like "giggled," "cried," or "shivered." Sense words help us imagine, like "crunchy," "sparkly," or "loud." They make stories come alive!
teachImage: Five cartoon characters each representing a sense: one touching a fluffy cat, one smelling a flower, one hearing a bird, one tasting an apple, one seeing a rainbow.
exampleBody: Read this: The warm sun melted the icy puddle. The word "warm" helps us feel the heat. The word "icy" helps us feel the cold. These words paint a picture in our minds!
exampleImage: A cartoon sun with a warm smile shining down on a melting icy puddle, with wavy heat lines and sparkly ice crystals.
tipBody: When you read a story or poem, slow down and ask: How does this word make me feel? Can I picture it, hear it, or feel it? Those are the special words!
tipImage: A cartoon child sitting under a tree with closed eyes, imagining scenes from a book floating around: a crackling fire, soft rain, a sweet cookie.
practiceBody: Wonderful! You can find words that show feelings and senses. Let's practice finding these special words in some questions!
practiceImage: A cartoon child painting the word "sparkle" on a canvas, with the letters actually sparkling and glowing.

title: Stories vs. Information Books
introBody: Did you know there are different kinds of books? Some books tell make-believe stories. Other books give us real facts and information. Let's learn how to tell them apart!
introImage: Two cartoon books side by side: one with a dragon and castle on the cover, and one with a real photo of a dolphin and the title "Ocean Facts."
teachBody: Storybooks have characters, adventures, and a beginning, middle, and end. Information books teach us real facts about a topic. They might have photos, headings, and labels!
teachImage: A cartoon child holding up two books and comparing them, with a thought bubble showing a fairy tale castle on one side and a diagram of a butterfly life cycle on the other.
exampleBody: Book A: "Once upon a time, a frog became a prince." Book B: "Frogs live near ponds and eat bugs." Book A is a story because it is make-believe. Book B gives information because it has real facts!
exampleImage: A cartoon frog wearing a tiny crown next to a real-looking cartoon frog sitting on a lily pad, with labels "Story" and "Information" above them.
tipBody: Ask yourself: Is this real or make-believe? Does it teach me facts or tell a story? If it has "once upon a time," it is probably a story!
tipImage: A cartoon child at a library sorting books into two bins labeled "Stories" and "Information" with a happy expression.
practiceBody: Now you know the difference between stories and information books! Let's try some practice questions. Think about what makes each one special!
practiceImage: A cartoon child reading in a cozy library nook surrounded by both storybooks and information books on shelves.

title: Who Is Telling the Story?
introBody: Every story has a narrator. That is the person or character telling the story. Sometimes a character in the story tells it, and sometimes someone outside the story does!
introImage: A cartoon child sitting in a big storytelling chair with a spotlight on them, holding an open book and gesturing dramatically.
teachHeading: How Do We Know Who Is Telling It?
teachBody: If you see words like "I" and "my," a character in the story is telling it. If you see words like "he," "she," or "they," someone outside the story is telling it!
teachImage: Two cartoon speech bubbles: one says "I found a kitten!" with a girl's face, and the other says "She found a kitten" with a narrator icon outside the scene.
exampleBody: Read this: "I walked to the park with my mom. We saw a butterfly." Who is telling the story? The word "I" tells us a character is the narrator. The child is telling the story!
exampleImage: A cartoon child walking with their mom in a park, with a speech bubble saying "I saw a butterfly!" and a butterfly nearby.
tipBody: Look for the words "I" and "my" to find out if a character is telling the story. If you do not see those words, someone else is the narrator!
tipImage: A cartoon magnifying glass zooming in on the words "I" and "my" highlighted in yellow on a book page.
practiceBody: Great work! You can figure out who is telling the story. Let's practice finding the narrator in some questions!
practiceImage: A cartoon child wearing a director's beret and pointing at different characters, deciding who the narrator is.

title: Pictures Help Tell the Story
introBody: The pictures in a book are not just pretty. They help tell the story too! We can learn about characters, where they are, and what is happening just by looking at the pictures.
introImage: A cartoon child leaning close to a picture book, pointing at an illustration of a cat climbing a tree.
teachBody: Pictures can show us how a character feels, what the weather is like, or what a place looks like. Look at the pictures AND read the words together to understand the whole story!
teachImage: A split cartoon scene: on the left, words say "The dog was sad," and on the right, the illustration shows a droopy-eared cartoon dog with a frown.
exampleBody: The story says: "Lily went outside." The picture shows her wearing a raincoat and boots with puddles everywhere. The picture tells us it is raining, even though the words do not say it!
exampleImage: A cartoon girl in a yellow raincoat and red boots splashing in puddles, with rain falling and gray clouds overhead.
tipBody: Always look at the pictures before, during, and after you read. They give you extra clues about the characters, setting, and events that the words might not tell you!
tipImage: A cartoon child with a pair of binoculars looking closely at a colorful illustration in a big picture book.
practiceBody: Awesome! You know that pictures and words work together to tell a story. Let's practice using pictures to answer questions!
practiceImage: A cartoon child surrounded by floating illustrations of story scenes: a castle, a friendly dragon, a sunny meadow.

title: Comparing Characters in Stories
introBody: Have you ever noticed that characters in different stories sometimes do similar things? We can compare them to see what is the same and what is different!
introImage: Two cartoon characters from different stories standing side by side: a girl with a basket and a boy with a backpack, connected by a line labeled "compare."
teachBody: Think about what each character does, how they feel, and what happens to them. What is the same? What is different? That is comparing and contrasting!
teachImage: A cartoon Venn diagram with two overlapping circles, each with a different character inside, and shared traits in the middle area.
exampleBody: Story 1: Sam was brave and climbed a tall tree. Story 2: Rosa was brave and crossed a wobbly bridge. Both Sam and Rosa were brave! But they did different brave things. That is how we compare!
exampleImage: A split scene showing a cartoon boy climbing a tree on the left and a cartoon girl crossing a rope bridge on the right, both looking brave.
tipBody: Use the words "same" and "different" to compare characters. Ask: How are they alike? How are they different? Think about their feelings, actions, and what happens to them!
tipImage: A cartoon child holding two puppets and looking back and forth between them with a thinking expression.
practiceBody: You are a great story detective! Let's compare some characters in the practice questions. Look for what is the same and what is different!
practiceImage: Two cartoon story characters shaking hands in front of their two different storybooks, showing they have things in common.

title: Questions About Real Things
introBody: When we read books about real things, we can ask questions too! Asking questions helps us learn new facts. The answers are right there in the text!
introImage: A cartoon child reading a book about planets, with question mark bubbles floating up: "How big is the sun?" and "Why is Mars red?"
teachHeading: How Do We Find Answers in Facts?
teachBody: Just like in stories, we can ask who, what, where, when, why, and how. Then we look in the text to find the answer. The facts are written right there for us!
teachImage: A cartoon magnifying glass hovering over a page about butterflies, with key facts highlighted in different colors.
exampleBody: Read this: Penguins live in cold places. They cannot fly, but they are great swimmers. Question: Can penguins fly? Answer: No, they cannot fly. We found the answer in the text!
exampleImage: A cartoon penguin diving into icy blue water with a splash, surrounded by snow and ice.
tipBody: Read the question carefully first. Then go back to the text and look for the sentence that answers it. The key words in the question will help you find the right spot!
tipImage: A cartoon child pointing at a sentence in a nonfiction book with a big smile, with a light bulb above their head.
practiceBody: You are ready to find facts in informational text! Let's try some practice questions. Read carefully and find the answers!
practiceImage: A cartoon child sitting at a desk with an open book about animals, surrounded by fun fact cards floating in the air.

title: Finding the Main Topic
introBody: Every informational text has a main topic. That is the big idea the whole text is about. We can also find key details that tell us more about the topic!
introImage: A cartoon child looking at a big poster with a tree diagram: the trunk says "Main Topic" and the branches say "Detail, Detail, Detail."
teachHeading: How Do We Find the Main Topic?
teachBody: Ask yourself: What is this whole text about? The main topic is the big subject. The details are smaller facts that support it. The title often gives us a big clue!
teachImage: A cartoon detective cat reading a book titled "All About Dogs" with a magnifying glass over the title, with dog-related details floating around.
exampleBody: Read this: Apples can be red, green, or yellow. They grow on trees. People eat apples in pies and as snacks. The main topic is apples! The details tell us about their colors, where they grow, and how we eat them.
exampleImage: A cartoon apple tree with red, green, and yellow apples, next to a pie and a child eating an apple as a snack.
tipBody: Look at the title and pictures first. They usually tell you the main topic. Then read the details to learn more. Ask: What do all these facts have in common?
tipImage: A cartoon child circling the title of a book with a big crayon, with arrows pointing to key details on the page.
practiceBody: You can find the main topic! Let's practice with some questions. Think about the big idea and the details that go with it!
practiceImage: A cartoon child holding a magnifying glass over a book, with the words "Main Topic" glowing on the page.

title: Connecting Ideas in a Text
introBody: In informational texts, ideas and events are connected. One thing might cause another. Two people might do something together. Let's learn to find these connections!
introImage: Two cartoon puzzle pieces clicking together, one showing rain clouds and the other showing flowers growing.
teachHeading: What Kinds of Connections Are There?
teachBody: Things can be connected in many ways. Rain helps flowers grow. A doctor helps a sick person get better. When we see how ideas go together, we understand the text better!
teachImage: A cartoon chain of links, each link showing a connected event: seed, rain, sprout, flower, in a sequence.
exampleBody: Read this: The weather got very cold. The pond froze into ice. Connection: The cold weather caused the pond to freeze! One event led to the other. See how they are connected?
exampleImage: A cartoon thermometer dropping low next to a pond turning from liquid water to shiny ice, with snowflakes falling.
tipBody: Look for clue words like "because," "so," "then," and "after." These words help you see how two things are connected in the text!
tipImage: A cartoon child stringing beads on a necklace, each bead labeled with a connecting word: "because," "so," "then."
practiceBody: Awesome! You can find connections between ideas. Let's try some practice questions. Look for how things are linked together!
practiceImage: A cartoon child drawing arrows between connected pictures on a bulletin board, like a mini detective.

title: Learning New Words in Facts
introBody: When we read about real things, we sometimes find words we do not know. We can ask questions and use clues in the text to figure out what those words mean!
introImage: A cartoon child scratching their head while looking at a new word in a book, with a light bulb slowly turning on above.
teachHeading: How Do We Figure Out New Words?
teachBody: Look at the words and sentences around the new word. They give you clues! You can also look at pictures for help. Ask: What would make sense here?
teachImage: A cartoon word "hibernate" in a sentence with arrows pointing to clue words around it: "bears," "sleep," "winter."
exampleBody: Read this: The caterpillar spins a cocoon. It stays inside until it becomes a butterfly. What is a cocoon? The text says the caterpillar stays inside it, so a cocoon must be a covering or a shell!
exampleImage: A cartoon caterpillar spinning a green cocoon on a branch, with a thought bubble showing a butterfly emerging.
tipBody: When you see a tricky word, do not skip it! Read the sentence again. The other words are clues to help you figure out the meaning. You are a word detective!
tipImage: A cartoon child wearing a detective hat, using a magnifying glass to look at a glowing word in a book.
practiceBody: You are a word detective! Let's practice figuring out new words. Use the clues in the text to find the meanings!
practiceImage: A cartoon child proudly adding a new word to a colorful word wall in a classroom.

title: Using Text Features
introBody: Informational books have special tools to help us find facts. Headings, tables of contents, glossaries, and icons are like signs that guide us through the book!
introImage: A cartoon child opening a book with labeled arrows pointing to a heading, a table of contents, and a glossary.
teachBody: A heading tells us what a section is about. A table of contents lists what is in the book. A glossary tells us what words mean. Icons are little pictures that give us clues!
teachImage: Four cartoon signs on a path: one says "Heading," one shows a table of contents page, one says "Glossary," and one has a little info icon.
exampleBody: You want to know what page has information about lizards. Where do you look? The table of contents! It lists all the topics and their page numbers. It is like a map for your book!
exampleImage: A cartoon child pointing at a table of contents page, with their finger on a line that says "Lizards ... page 12."
tipBody: Before you start reading, look at the headings and pictures first. They give you a preview of what you will learn. Use the glossary if you see a word you do not know!
tipImage: A cartoon child flipping to the glossary at the back of a book, with a speech bubble saying "Oh, that is what it means!"
practiceBody: Great job! You know how to use text features to find information. Let's practice finding facts using headings, tables of contents, and more!
practiceImage: A cartoon child navigating through a book like a treasure map, with text features lighting up as helpful landmarks.

title: Pictures vs. Words
introHeading: What Do Pictures and Words Each Tell Us?
introBody: In informational books, we get information from both pictures and words. Sometimes the picture shows us something the words do not say. Let's learn to tell the difference!
introImage: A split cartoon scene: on the left, a paragraph about dolphins, and on the right, a labeled diagram of a dolphin showing its fin, tail, and blowhole.
teachHeading: How Are Pictures and Words Different?
teachBody: The words tell us facts in sentences. The pictures show us what things look like. Sometimes a picture gives us extra information that the words do not mention!
teachImage: A cartoon child holding a magnifying glass over a page, comparing the text to a photo of a tree with labels pointing to roots, trunk, and leaves.
exampleBody: The words say: "Birds build nests." The picture shows a bird using twigs, grass, and feathers. The picture tells us what materials birds use, even though the words did not say it!
exampleImage: A cartoon bird building a nest with twigs and grass in a tree branch, with labeled arrows pointing to "twigs," "grass," and "feathers."
tipBody: When answering a question, ask yourself: Did I learn this from the words or from the picture? Both are important, but they can tell us different things!
tipImage: A cartoon child looking at a book page with two thought bubbles: one shows words and the other shows a picture, with a question mark between them.
practiceBody: You can tell the difference between what pictures and words teach us! Let's practice figuring out where information comes from!
practiceImage: A cartoon child giving a presentation with a poster that has both words and pictures, pointing to each one.

title: Using Pictures to Understand Ideas
introBody: Illustrations, photos, and diagrams in informational texts help us understand the main ideas. When we use pictures and words together, we learn even more!
introImage: A cartoon child looking at a big colorful diagram of the water cycle with labels for evaporation, clouds, and rain.
teachHeading: How Do Pictures Help Us Understand?
teachBody: Pictures can show us what something looks like, how something works, or how things are connected. Use the pictures and the words together to get the full picture!
teachImage: A cartoon book page about volcanoes, with a cross-section diagram showing magma, the crater, and lava flowing down.
exampleBody: The text says: "A caterpillar changes into a butterfly." The picture shows the stages: egg, caterpillar, cocoon, butterfly. The picture helps us see all the steps of the change!
exampleImage: A cartoon diagram showing the butterfly life cycle in four stages arranged in a circle with arrows connecting them.
tipBody: Always study the pictures carefully. Look at labels, captions, and arrows. They give you extra information that helps you understand the key ideas in the text!
tipImage: A cartoon child tracing an arrow in a diagram with their finger, with a light bulb appearing above their head.
practiceBody: You know how to use pictures and words together to learn! Let's try some practice questions about using illustrations to understand ideas!
practiceImage: A cartoon child surrounded by informational book pages with diagrams, photos, and labeled illustrations floating around.

title: Finding the Author's Reasons
introHeading: Why Does the Author Say That?
introBody: Authors write things for a reason. When they make a point, they give us reasons to help us understand why. Let's learn to find those reasons!
introImage: A cartoon author at a desk writing, with thought bubbles containing reasons: "because," "for example," "this shows."
teachHeading: How Do We Find the Reasons?
teachBody: An author might say: "Helmets are important." Then they give reasons like: "They protect your head." The reasons explain why the author's point makes sense!
teachImage: A cartoon child wearing a helmet on a bike, with a speech bubble listing reasons: "Protects your head" and "Keeps you safe."
exampleBody: Read this: You should drink water every day. Water keeps your body healthy and gives you energy. The author's point is: Drink water. The reasons are: It keeps you healthy and gives you energy!
exampleImage: A cartoon child happily drinking a glass of water, with icons showing a healthy heart and a lightning bolt for energy.
tipBody: First find the author's main point. Then look for sentences that explain why. Words like "because" and "so" help you spot the reasons!
tipImage: A cartoon child underlining a main point in red and the reasons in blue on a book page, with a proud smile.
practiceBody: Great work! You can find the reasons an author gives to support their points. Let's practice with some questions!
practiceImage: A cartoon child holding a list with a big point at the top and supporting reasons below, connected by arrows.

title: Comparing Two Texts
introBody: Sometimes we read two texts about the same topic. They might say some of the same things and some different things. Let's learn to compare them!
introImage: Two cartoon books about dogs side by side, each open to different pages with some overlapping facts highlighted.
teachHeading: How Do We Compare Two Texts?
teachBody: Read both texts carefully. Ask: What facts are the same in both? What facts are different? Make a list or use a Venn diagram to organize what you find!
teachImage: A cartoon Venn diagram on a whiteboard with two overlapping circles labeled "Text 1" and "Text 2," with facts written in each section.
exampleBody: Text 1: Bees make honey and live in hives. Text 2: Bees help flowers grow and live in hives. Both texts say bees live in hives. But one talks about honey and the other talks about flowers!
exampleImage: Two cartoon panels: one showing bees making honey in a hive, and another showing bees buzzing around flowers, with a checkmark on "live in hives" in both.
tipBody: As you read each text, write down the main facts. Then compare your lists. Circle the facts that are the same and underline the ones that are different!
tipImage: A cartoon child with two lists of facts, drawing circles around matching facts and underlining the different ones.
practiceBody: You are great at comparing texts! Let's practice finding what is the same and different in some questions!
practiceImage: A cartoon child holding two informational books triumphantly, with a check mark between them showing they compared successfully.

title: What Makes a Sentence?
introBody: A sentence has special rules! It starts with a capital letter and ends with a special mark like a period, question mark, or exclamation point. Let's learn these rules!
introImage: A cartoon sentence on a banner: "The cat is fluffy." with a sparkly capital T at the start and a glowing period at the end.
teachBody: Every sentence starts with a capital letter. It ends with a period, a question mark, or an exclamation point. These marks tell us when the sentence is done!
teachImage: Three cartoon signs held by animals: a turtle holding a period sign, a curious owl holding a question mark, and an excited monkey holding an exclamation point.
exampleBody: Look at this: "the dog runs fast" Is this a correct sentence? No! It needs a capital T at the beginning and a period at the end: "The dog runs fast." Now it is a real sentence!
exampleImage: A cartoon sentence being fixed: a lowercase "the" getting a capital T crown and a period being stamped at the end by a friendly robot.
tipBody: Check every sentence for two things: Does it start with a capital letter? Does it end with a period, question mark, or exclamation point? If yes to both, it is a proper sentence!
tipImage: A cartoon child with a checklist: "Capital letter? Check! Ending mark? Check!" with green checkmarks next to each.
practiceBody: You know the rules of a sentence! Let's practice spotting capital letters and ending punctuation in some questions!
practiceImage: A cartoon child placing a capital letter block at the start and a period block at the end of a word train.

title: Blending Sounds Into Words
introBody: We can put sounds together to make words! When we blend sounds like /s/ /t/ /o/ /p/, we get the word "stop." Let's practice blending sounds into words!
introImage: Cartoon letter tiles s, t, o, p sliding together on a table to form the word "stop," with a friendly child watching.
teachBody: Say each sound slowly, then say them faster and faster until they become a word. Some words start with two consonants together, like "bl" in "black" or "st" in "star."
teachImage: A cartoon blender with letters dropping in and a word popping out the top, like a fun kitchen smoothie machine.
exampleBody: Let's blend! /f/ /l/ /a/ /g/. Say it slowly: fff-lll-aaa-ggg. Now say it fast: flag! You just blended a word with a consonant blend "fl" at the beginning. Great job!
exampleImage: A cartoon child sounding out letters with speech bubbles: "/f/ /l/ /a/ /g/" leading to a big flag waving.
tipBody: Start slow and then speed up. Say each sound, then push them together like a train connecting its cars. Soon the sounds will turn into a word you know!
tipImage: Cartoon train cars, each with a letter on it, connecting together on a track, forming a word at the station.
practiceBody: You are a sound blending pro! Let's practice blending sounds into words. Listen to the sounds and put them together!
practiceImage: A cartoon child with headphones, smiling as sound waves merge into a word bubble above their head.

title: Beginning, Middle, and End Sounds
introBody: Every word has sounds at the beginning, in the middle, and at the end. When we listen carefully, we can hear each sound. Let's practice picking out sounds in words!
introImage: A cartoon word "cat" with three colored sections: blue "c" at the start, yellow "a" in the middle, and red "t" at the end.
teachBody: The beginning sound is the first sound you hear. The middle sound is the vowel in the center. The end sound is the last sound. In "dog," the sounds are /d/, /o/, /g/!
teachImage: A cartoon dog standing between two signposts, one labeled "Beginning /d/" and one labeled "End /g/," with the dog wearing a shirt with the vowel "o" on it.
exampleBody: Say the word "sun." What is the first sound? /s/! What is the middle sound? /u/! What is the last sound? /n/! You found all three sounds!
exampleImage: A cartoon sun with three rays highlighted in different colors, each labeled with a sound: /s/, /u/, /n/.
tipBody: Say the word slowly and stretch it out like taffy. Listen for each sound one at a time. The middle sound is usually a vowel: a, e, i, o, or u!
tipImage: A cartoon child stretching a piece of taffy with letters on it, pulling the word apart to hear each sound.
practiceBody: You can hear the sounds in words! Let's practice picking out beginning, middle, and end sounds in some questions!
practiceImage: A cartoon child cupping their ear and listening carefully, with three sound bubbles in different colors floating nearby.

title: Breaking Words Into Sounds
introBody: We can take a word and break it into all of its sounds. This is called segmenting! It is like taking a word apart piece by piece. Let's try it!
introImage: A cartoon word "ship" being gently pulled apart into three sound pieces: /sh/, /i/, /p/ by a friendly cartoon child.
teachBody: Say the word slowly. Count each sound you hear. The word "map" has three sounds: /m/ /a/ /p/. Hold up a finger for each sound to keep track!
teachImage: A cartoon child holding up three fingers, one for each sound in a word, with sound labels floating by each finger.
exampleBody: Let's segment the word "frog." Say it slowly: /f/ /r/ /o/ /g/. How many sounds? Four! Each sound is a piece of the word. You just segmented like a pro!
exampleImage: A cartoon frog sitting on four lily pads, each lily pad labeled with a sound: /f/, /r/, /o/, /g/.
tipBody: Use your fingers or counters to count the sounds. Say the word slowly and tap once for each new sound. Remember, some letters make one sound together, like "sh"!
tipImage: A cartoon child tapping colorful counters on a table, one for each sound in a word, with the word displayed above.
practiceBody: You are a word segmenting star! Let's practice breaking words into sounds. Say each word slowly and count the sounds!
practiceImage: A cartoon child smashing a word piñata, with individual sounds falling out like candy.

title: Reading Aloud With Expression
introBody: Good readers do not just say the words. They read with feeling! They make their voice go up for questions and loud for exciting parts. Let's practice reading aloud!
introImage: A cartoon child on a stage reading from a book into a microphone with dramatic hand gestures and a spotlight.
teachHeading: How Do We Read With Expression?
teachBody: Change your voice to match the words! Read slowly when something is sad. Read fast when something is exciting. Make your voice go up at the end of a question!
teachImage: A cartoon volume dial going from quiet to loud, with different cartoon faces showing expressions: sad, happy, surprised, excited.
exampleBody: Read this with expression: "Oh no!" cried the rabbit. "My carrot is gone!" Did you make your voice sound worried? The exclamation points tell us to read with strong feeling!
exampleImage: A cartoon rabbit with wide eyes and paws on its cheeks looking at an empty garden patch, with speech bubbles showing exclamation marks.
tipBody: Look at the punctuation marks! A period means stop calmly. A question mark means your voice goes up. An exclamation point means read with excitement or strong feeling!
tipImage: A cartoon child reading a book with different punctuation marks glowing in different colors, each one changing the child's expression.
practiceBody: You read with great expression! Let's practice reading aloud. Pay attention to the punctuation and let your voice bring the words to life!
practiceImage: A cartoon child reading a book aloud to a group of stuffed animals, all looking engaged and entertained.

title: Using Clues to Read Words
introBody: Sometimes we get stuck on a word while reading. But the words around it can help us! We can use context clues to figure out the tricky word and check if it makes sense.
introImage: A cartoon child reading a book and pausing at a tricky word, with surrounding words glowing as helpful clues.
teachBody: If you are not sure about a word, read the rest of the sentence. Think: What word would make sense here? Then check: Does that word look right and sound right?
teachImage: A cartoon sentence with a blank where a word should be, surrounded by clue words with arrows pointing to the blank: "The ___ barked loudly."
exampleBody: Read this: The baby was tired and began to yawn. If you got stuck on "yawn," the clues "tired" and "baby" help you know it means to open your mouth wide when sleepy!
exampleImage: A cartoon baby in pajamas yawning with closed eyes, sitting in a crib with a teddy bear and a moon in the window.
tipBody: If a word does not make sense, stop and try again. Look at the first letter, read the rest of the sentence, and ask: What word fits? Then reread to make sure it sounds right!
tipImage: A cartoon child rereading a sentence with a finger under the words, with a green checkmark appearing when the word makes sense.
practiceBody: You can use context clues to figure out tricky words! Let's practice using the words around a word to help us understand!
practiceImage: A cartoon child jumping over a hurdle labeled "Tricky Word" with context clue stepping stones helping them along.

title: Figuring Out Unknown Words
introBody: Sometimes we find words we have never seen before. Other times a word can mean more than one thing! We can use different strategies to figure out what words mean.
introImage: A cartoon child dressed as an explorer with a safari hat, using a compass that points to a glowing unknown word.
teachHeading: How Do We Figure Out Words?
teachBody: We can use clues from the sentence, look at parts of the word, or think about other meanings. Some words, like "bat," can mean a flying animal or something you hit a ball with!
teachImage: A cartoon word "bat" with two arrows: one pointing to a cute cartoon bat flying, and the other pointing to a baseball bat.
exampleBody: Read this: She picked up a bat and hit the ball over the fence. What does "bat" mean here? The words "hit the ball" tell us it is a baseball bat, not a flying animal!
exampleImage: A cartoon girl swinging a baseball bat and hitting a ball high in the air at a sunny baseball field.
tipBody: When you find an unknown word, try three things: Read the sentence for clues. Look at the parts of the word. Think about what meaning makes sense in the sentence!
tipImage: A cartoon toolbox labeled "Word Tools" with three items inside: a magnifying glass, word parts, and a context clue flashlight.
practiceBody: You are a word explorer! Let's practice figuring out unknown words and words with more than one meaning. Use your word tools!
practiceImage: A cartoon child standing proudly on top of a mountain of words, planting a flag that says "I Know Words!"

title: Context Clues for Words
introBody: The sentence around a word is like a clue box. The other words in the sentence can help us figure out what a tricky word means. Let's learn to use context clues!
introImage: A cartoon sentence with a tricky word in the center, surrounded by glowing helper words arranged like a frame.
teachBody: Context clues are the words and sentences around a word you do not know. They help you guess the meaning. Read the whole sentence to find these helpful clues!
teachImage: A cartoon treasure map where X marks the unknown word, and the paths to it are labeled with context clue words.
exampleBody: Read this: The enormous elephant was the biggest animal at the zoo. What does "enormous" mean? The word "biggest" is a context clue. Enormous means very, very big!
exampleImage: A cartoon elephant towering over other zoo animals, with a sign saying "ENORMOUS" and a smaller sign saying "biggest" with an arrow between them.
tipBody: Read the sentence one more time and skip the hard word. Then ask: What word would make sense here? Use the other words as clues to figure it out!
tipImage: A cartoon child jumping over a gap in a sentence where the unknown word should be, then filling it in with the right meaning.
practiceBody: You are great at using context clues! Let's practice finding the meaning of words by looking at the sentences around them!
practiceImage: A cartoon child with a flashlight illuminating context clue words that light up the path to an unknown word's meaning.

title: Word Parts Give Clues
introBody: Some words have special parts added to the beginning or end. These parts change the meaning! When we know what the parts mean, we can figure out new words.
introImage: A cartoon word being built with blocks: "un" snapping onto "happy" to make "unhappy," with a child assembling them.
teachBody: A prefix goes at the beginning of a word. "Un" means not, so "unhappy" means not happy. A suffix goes at the end. "Ful" means full of, so "hopeful" means full of hope!
teachImage: A cartoon chart showing prefixes on the left and suffixes on the right, with example words in the middle, like a word sandwich.
exampleBody: What does "unkind" mean? The prefix "un" means not. The word "kind" means nice. So "unkind" means not kind, or not nice. See how the word part changed the meaning?
exampleImage: A cartoon before-and-after: a smiling face labeled "kind" next to a frowning face labeled "unkind," with "un" being attached like a sticker.
tipBody: When you see a long word, look for parts you know. Do you see "un," "re," or "ful"? Those parts give you a big clue about what the whole word means!
tipImage: A cartoon child with scissors carefully cutting a word into its parts: prefix, root word, and suffix, each on a different colored piece.
practiceBody: You can use word parts to figure out meanings! Let's practice looking at prefixes and suffixes. Break the words apart and find the clues!
practiceImage: A cartoon child building words with colorful snap-together blocks, each block labeled with a word part.

title: Root Words and Their Forms
introBody: A root word is the main part of a word. We can add endings to change it! The word "play" can become "plays," "played," or "playing." The root word is always "play!"
introImage: A cartoon tree with "play" on the trunk and branches labeled "plays," "played," "playing," and "player."
teachBody: We add endings like -s, -ed, -ing, and -er to root words. These endings change the word a little. "Walk" becomes "walks," "walked," and "walking." The root is still "walk!"
teachImage: A cartoon conveyor belt with the root word "jump" at the start, and different endings being stamped on: "jumps," "jumped," "jumping."
exampleBody: What is the root word of "running"? Take away the -ing ending: run! What is the root word of "played"? Take away the -ed ending: play! Finding the root word is easy!
exampleImage: A cartoon child peeling off an "-ing" sticker from the word "running" to reveal the root word "run" underneath.
tipBody: To find the root word, cover up the ending with your finger. What word is left? That is the root word! Remember: -s, -ed, -ing, and -er are common endings.
tipImage: A cartoon child covering the ending of a word with their thumb, revealing the root word, with a proud smile.
practiceBody: You can find root words! Let's practice identifying root words and their different forms. Look for the base word hiding inside!
practiceImage: A cartoon garden with root words planted in the ground and different word forms growing out like flowers.

title: Understanding Word Meanings
introBody: Words have special relationships! Some words are similar, some are opposite, and some go together in groups. Understanding these connections helps us use words better.
introImage: A cartoon web of connected words with lines between them: "hot" connected to "cold," "big" connected to "small," "happy" connected to "glad."
teachBody: Some words mean almost the same thing, like "big" and "large." Some words are opposites, like "hot" and "cold." Learning how words connect helps us choose the best word!
teachImage: A cartoon balance scale with "big" on one side and "large" balanced equally, and another scale with "hot" and "cold" on opposite sides.
exampleBody: Which word is the opposite of "fast"? Is it "quick" or "slow"? The answer is "slow"! Fast and slow are opposites because they mean completely different things.
exampleImage: A cartoon race between a speedy rabbit labeled "fast" and a slow turtle labeled "slow," showing they are opposites.
tipBody: Think about how words are connected. Are they similar? Are they opposites? Do they belong to the same group? These connections help you understand and remember new words!
tipImage: A cartoon child connecting words with colorful yarn on a wall, making a word relationship map.
practiceBody: You understand how words are related! Let's practice finding word connections. Think about how words go together or are different!
practiceImage: A cartoon child surrounded by word cards, happily sorting them into groups and connecting them with arrows.

title: Sorting Words Into Groups
introBody: We can sort words into categories, just like sorting toys into bins! Words that go together help us understand what a group or category is all about.
introImage: A cartoon child sorting word cards into colorful bins labeled "Animals," "Foods," and "Colors."
teachBody: Think about what the words have in common. "Apple, banana, grape" are all fruits! "Dog, cat, fish" are all animals! The category name tells us what the group is about.
teachImage: Three cartoon baskets: one with fruit words, one with animal words, and one with color words, each basket labeled with its category.
exampleBody: Where does "carrot" go? Is it a fruit, a vegetable, or an animal? A carrot is a vegetable! It goes in the vegetable group with broccoli and peas. Categories help us organize words!
exampleImage: A cartoon carrot with legs walking toward a basket labeled "Vegetables" where broccoli and peas are already waiting inside.
tipBody: Ask yourself: What do these words have in common? If they are all things you can eat, they might be foods. If they all move and breathe, they might be animals!
tipImage: A cartoon child with a thinking cap on, looking at word cards and organizing them on a table into neat groups.
practiceBody: You are a great word sorter! Let's practice putting words into categories. Think about what the words have in common!
practiceImage: A cartoon child proudly standing next to perfectly sorted word bins, with a gold star sticker on their shirt.

title: Defining Words by Type
introHeading: What Kind of Thing Is It?
introBody: When we define a word, we tell what category it belongs to and what makes it special. A "robin" is a bird that has a red chest. Let's learn to define words this way!
introImage: A cartoon robin sitting on a branch with two labels: "Category: Bird" and "Special: Red chest."
teachBody: First, say what category the word belongs to. Then tell what makes it special. A "hammer" is a tool that you use to hit nails. Category: tool. Special: hits nails!
teachImage: A cartoon hammer with an arrow pointing to a toolbox labeled "Category: Tool" and another arrow to a nail labeled "Special: Hits nails."
exampleBody: What is a "daisy"? A daisy is a flower. That is the category. It has white petals and a yellow center. Those are the special things about it. A daisy is a flower with white petals and a yellow center!
exampleImage: A cartoon daisy with labels pointing to its white petals and yellow center, next to a sign saying "Category: Flower."
tipBody: When defining a word, first say the group it belongs to: "It is a type of..." Then add what makes it different from others in the group: "It has..." or "It is used for..."
tipImage: A cartoon child filling in a definition template on a whiteboard: "A ___ is a ___ that ___."
practiceBody: You can define words by category and special features! Let's practice describing what things are and what makes them unique!
practiceImage: A cartoon child playing a guessing game, giving clues about a word: "It is an animal. It has stripes. It is a zebra!"

title: Words in Real Life
introBody: The words we learn in books are the same words we use in real life! Let's learn to connect words to things we see, feel, and do every day.
introImage: A cartoon child walking through a neighborhood, with word labels floating above real things: "tree," "mailbox," "sidewalk," "sunny."
teachHeading: How Do Words Connect to Real Life?
teachBody: When you learn the word "cozy," think about when you feel cozy. Maybe wrapped in a blanket with hot cocoa! Connecting words to real life helps us remember and use them.
teachImage: A cartoon child snuggled in a blanket with hot cocoa, with the word "cozy" floating above in warm letters.
exampleBody: The word is "chilly." When do you feel chilly in real life? Maybe when you go outside without a jacket on a cold morning! That chilly feeling helps you understand what the word means.
exampleImage: A cartoon child hugging themselves on a cold morning without a jacket, with small shiver lines and the word "chilly" in icy blue letters.
tipBody: When you learn a new word, think of a time in your life when you could use it. Try to use new words when you talk to friends and family. That is how words become yours!
tipImage: A cartoon child using a new word in a conversation at dinner, with the family looking impressed and a speech bubble showing the word.
practiceBody: You can connect words to real life! Let's practice thinking about when and where we use different words every day!
practiceImage: A cartoon child pointing at things in a park and naming them with descriptive words, like "tall" tree and "fluffy" clouds.

title: Shades of Word Meaning
introHeading: Words Can Be Strong or Gentle!
introBody: Some words mean almost the same thing but with different strength. "Walk," "march," and "tiptoe" are all ways of moving, but they feel very different! Let's explore!
introImage: Three cartoon children in a row: one walking calmly, one marching with high knees, and one tiptoeing quietly.
teachBody: Just like colors have shades from light to dark, words have shades too! "Happy," "glad," and "thrilled" all mean happy, but "thrilled" is a much stronger feeling!
teachImage: A cartoon paint strip going from light to dark, with words at each shade: "glad" (light), "happy" (medium), "thrilled" (dark/strong).
exampleBody: Which is stronger: "said" or "shouted"? "Shouted" is much stronger! When you say something, you use a normal voice. When you shout, you are very loud! Shouted is a stronger shade of said.
exampleImage: Two cartoon scenes: a child calmly saying "hello" with small letters, and another child shouting "HELLO" with big bold letters and sound waves.
tipBody: Try acting out the words! Walk, then march, then stomp. Can you feel the difference? Acting out words helps you understand their different shades of meaning!
tipImage: A cartoon child acting out three different walking styles with exaggerated movements, looking like they are having fun.
practiceBody: You understand shades of meaning! Let's practice comparing words to see which ones are stronger, softer, bigger, or smaller!
practiceImage: A cartoon child arranging word cards in order from weakest to strongest, like a staircase going up.

title: Using New Words You Learn
introBody: Every day you learn new words from talking, reading, and listening. The more words you know, the better you can share your ideas. Let's practice using new words!
introImage: A cartoon child with a growing word garden, where each flower is a new word they have learned: "amazing," "discover," "adventure."
teachHeading: Where Do We Learn New Words?
teachBody: We learn new words from books, from teachers, from friends, and from family. When you hear a new word, try to use it. The more you use it, the better you will remember it!
teachImage: A cartoon child surrounded by word sources: an open book, a talking teacher, a friend whispering a word, and a parent reading aloud.
exampleBody: You learned the word "magnificent" from a story. It means really wonderful! Now use it: "The sunset was magnificent." See? You used a new word to describe something beautiful!
exampleImage: A cartoon child pointing at a gorgeous sunset over the ocean, with a speech bubble saying "Magnificent!" and stars in their eyes.
tipBody: Keep a list of new words you learn. Try to use one new word every day in a sentence. The more you practice, the more words you will know and the better reader you will become!
tipImage: A cartoon child writing a new word in a colorful word journal, with previously learned words stacked up like building blocks.
practiceBody: You are growing your word power! Let's practice using the new words you have learned. Pick the right word for each sentence!
practiceImage: A cartoon child flexing their arms, with word muscles growing bigger, surrounded by floating vocabulary words.
